panelReasoning: A device is called smart or intelligent because it embeds computation - a microcomputer (microprocessor plus memory and I/O) small enough to sit inside the device - whereas a sensor only transduces and confers no intelligence, 'Computer' is the vague superset, and 'Programmable' is an adjective that cannot follow 'contains within it a'. The source key D (Sensor) is repeated by legacy bank mirrors but is wrong on the merits, so the answer is Microcomputer.

Apply the two constraints separately: 'write-once' eliminates magneto-optical (rewritable) and CD-ROM (factory-pressed read-only, never user-written at all), leaving WORM disk and digital paper; 'latest' then eliminates WORM (early 1980s), leaving digital paper, the write-once optical medium of c.1990. The keyed D (CD-ROM) makes the question self-contradictory since CD-ROM is not write-once media.

C ('expensive') is not a correctness criterion at all and D is the classic B+C compound trap, so both fall; between the survivors, the binding constraint on whether purchased software will actually run is the host platform - CPU, OS, RAM and disk, i.e. the system requirements - making hardware compatibility the primary dependency and interoperability with other software secondary. The item has no source key, so the answer is derived.

UDP is a connectionless transport-layer protocol with no physical medium, so 'covers how many ft' is a category error and no truthful answer exists for the stem as written; the item also carries zero options and zero key. The only recoverable reading is that UDP is a corruption of UTP (100 m / ~328 ft segment limit), but that is a rewrite of the question rather than an answer to it, so the item must be repaired, not answered.

The item survives with a single option, 'Ethernet topology', which is a fragment of the stem rather than an answer, and it is false on its merits: FDDI is a dual counter-rotating token-passing fiber ring for campus/MAN backbones, precisely not an Ethernet topology. The inline_bold key simply marked the only text present; the intact twin (ce2016-q058, 'Which Ethernet topology do we use FDDI in?' with Bus/Ring/Tree/Star) shows the item must be rebuilt, not answered.

Residency is a caching decision: the kernel is kept memory-resident because it holds the most frequently invoked OS functions - it services every system call, interrupt and context switch, so swapping it out would fault on essentially every dispatch. Managing interrupt calls is something the kernel does, not the reason it must stay resident, and the intact four-option source item keys 'it is mostly used (called)', which is option B here.

Three independent defects make the item unrepairable in place: the stem's '3x8 decoder from three 1x2 decoders' is impossible and does not match the figure, which shows a 4x16 decoder built from five 2x4 decoders with inputs A,B,C,D; the options reference signals X,Y,Z and Y1,Y2,Y3 that appear nowhere in the figure; and options C and D are character-for-character identical, so no unique selection could exist even with a correct key.

The extracted table has only one data input plus a clock-edge column yet four rows whose next states (Q, Q, 0, 1) are self-contradictory for a single input and match neither JK (00->Q, 01->0, 10->1, 11->Q-bar) nor SR (11 invalid) - it reads as two single-input tables (T and D) merged after a column was lost. With a corrupted table and only two of four options surviving, the true answer may not even be listed, so nothing can be derived.
